title: Training vs Inference — Technical Differences
key_insight: Training requires gradients (backward pass), inference doesn't. This single difference explains most of the cost, memory, and speed differences between the two modes.
body:
## The Backward Pass: What Training Adds

```python
import torch

model = torch.nn.Linear(10, 1)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters())

# Training: forward + backward + update
x = torch.randn(32, 10)  # batch of 32 inputs
y = torch.randn(32, 1)   # true labels

# Forward pass (same as inference)
pred = model(x)

# Loss computation (only during training)
loss = torch.nn.MSELoss()(pred, y)

# Backward pass (ONLY during training)
# Computes gradients for every parameter
# Takes ~2x the memory of forward pass
loss.backward()
optimizer.step()   # updates weights
optimizer.zero_grad()

# --- vs ---

# Inference: forward pass only
with torch.no_grad():  # disable gradient tracking
    pred = model(x)    # uses ~40-60% less memory
    # No loss, no backward, no optimizer step
```

## Memory Differences

```python
# During training, memory holds:
# 1. Model weights              (e.g. 14GB for 7B param model in fp16)
# 2. Optimizer state (Adam: 2x) (e.g. 28GB)
# 3. Gradients                  (e.g. 14GB)
# 4. Activations (for backprop) (varies with batch size)
# Total: ~4-8x the model size

# During inference, memory holds:
# 1. Model weights only         (e.g. 14GB)
# 2. KV cache (for autoregressive) (depends on sequence length)
# Total: ~1-2x the model size
```

## Autoregressive Inference (LLMs)

```python
# Language model inference is sequential, not batched
# Each new token requires a full forward pass using all previous tokens

tokens = [tokenize('The weather is')]  # = [464, 4965, 318]

for step in range(max_new_tokens):
    with torch.no_grad():
        logits = model(tokens)        # forward pass with ALL previous tokens
    next_token = sample(logits[-1])   # pick next token
    tokens.append(next_token)         # extend sequence
    if next_token == END_TOKEN: break

# This is why LLMs are slow: N tokens = N sequential forward passes
# KV cache: store key/value vectors to avoid recomputing from scratch
```

## Batching at Inference

```python
# You can batch multiple requests together for efficiency:
batch_inputs = [request_1, request_2, request_3]  # pad to same length
with torch.no_grad():
    batch_outputs = model(batch_inputs)  # one forward pass, 3 results

# Tradeoff: batching reduces cost per request but increases latency
# Production systems tune batch size to balance throughput vs latency SLAs
```
common_mistakes: Forgetting torch.no_grad() during inference — wastes memory and compute tracking unused gradients
Trying to call loss.backward() during inference — there's no loss target, and it would crash
Assuming inference latency scales linearly with model size — batching and KV cache change the dynamics significantly
mini_exercise: In PyTorch, time a forward pass WITH torch.no_grad() vs WITHOUT on a medium-sized model. Measure peak memory usage with torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated(). What's the ratio?
real_world_usage: LLaMA.cpp compresses a 7B model to 4-bit precision (4-bit quantisation), dropping inference memory from ~14GB to ~4GB. This allows running the model on a laptop. The training of the original model still required 16-bit precision and multiple A100 GPUs. Training and inference hardware requirements are completely different.
explain_back_prompt: Why does training require more memory than inference? What is the backward pass, and why isn't it needed during inference? What is torch.no_grad() and why should you always use it during inference?

build_task.title: Measure the memory cost of gradients
build_task.context: Quantifying the cost of training vs inference makes the difference tangible.
steps: Create a moderately sized PyTorch model (3 linear layers)
Run inference with torch.no_grad() — record memory
Run a training step (forward + backward) — record memory
Print the model's parameter count: sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
build_task.expected_output: Memory ratio typically 2-4x for training vs inference. Parameter count printed.
build_task.starter_code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(512, 1024),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Linear(1024, 1024),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Linear(1024, 10),
)

params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
print(f'Parameters: {params:,}')

x = torch.randn(64, 512)

# Inference
torch.cuda.reset_peak_memory_stats()  # if using GPU
with torch.no_grad():
    out = model(x)
    inf_mem = torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated() / 1e6
    print(f'Inference memory: {inf_mem:.1f} MB')

# Training
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
y = torch.randint(0, 10, (64,))
torch.cuda.reset_peak_memory_stats()
out = model(x)
loss = loss_fn(out, y)
loss.backward()
train_mem = torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated() / 1e6
print(f'Training memory:  {train_mem:.1f} MB')
print(f'Ratio: {train_mem/inf_mem:.1f}x')
